Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَطَلِّقُوهُنَّ لِعِدَّتِهِنَّ وَأَحْصُوا۟ ٱلْعِدَّةَ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّكُمْ ۖ لَا تُخْرِجُوهُنَّ مِنۢ بُيُوتِهِنَّ وَلَا يَخْرُجْنَ إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِينَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ مُّبَيِّنَةٍ ۚ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُۥ ۚ لَا تَدْرِى لَعَلَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحْدِثُ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ أَمْرًا (١)
O Prophet! When you divorce women, divorce them at [the conclusion of] their term and calculate the term, and be wary of Allah, your Lord. Do not turn them out from their homes, nor shall they go out, unless they commit a gross indecency. These are Allah’s bounds, and whoever transgresses the bounds of Allah certainly wrongs himself. You never know maybe Allah will bring off something new later on. (1)

Content of Surah At-Talaq

The most important issue discussed in this Surah—as is evident from its name—is the matter of "Divorce" (Talaq), its rulings, characteristics, and consequences. This is followed by discussions regarding the Origin (Mabda') and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), the Prophethood of the Prophet, and glad tidings and warnings.

Accordingly, the content of this Surah can be divided into two main sections:

The first section consists of the first seven verses of the Surah, which speak about divorce and its related issues. It presents the details of this matter so precisely and delicately in short yet meaningful phrases that a person, upon observing them, finds himself completely well-informed.

The second section, which in reality serves as the motivation for implementing the first section, discusses the greatness of Allah, the grandeur of His Messenger's status, the reward of the righteous, and the punishment of the evildoers. It provides a cohesive framework to guarantee the implementation of this important social issue. Additionally, this Surah has another name, "Surat al-Nisa' al-Qusra" (the shorter Surah of Women), in contrast to the well-known Surah al-Nisa', which is "al-Nisa' al-Kubra" (the larger Surah of Women).

The Virtue of Reciting the Surah

In a hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

مَنْ قَرَأَ سُورَةَ الطَّلَاقِ مَاتَ عَلَى سُنَّةِ رَسُولِ اللَّهِ صلى الله عليه وآله

"Whoever recites Surah At-Talaq (and applies it in his life's programs) will die upon the Sunnah of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله." [1]

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 302.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ فَطَلِّقُوهُنَّ لِعِدَّتِهِنَّ وَأَحْصُوا الْعِدَّةَ وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ رَبَّكُمْ لَا تُخْرِجُوهُنَّ مِنْ بُيُوتِهِنَّ وَلَا يَخْرُجْنَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَأْتِينَ بِفَاحِشَةٍ مُبَيِّنَةٍ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ وَمَنْ يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ اللَّهِ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُ لَا تَدْرِي لَعَلَّ اللَّهَ يُحْدِثُ بَعْدَ ذَلِكَ أَمْرًا (1)

Commentary

Conditions of Divorce and Separation

We mentioned that the most important discussion in this Surah is that of divorce, which begins from its very first verse. The address is directed to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله as the great leader of the Muslims, and then a general ruling is expressed in the plural form, stating:

"O Prophet! When you [Muslims] divorce women, divorce them for [the commencement of] their waiting period!" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ فَطَلِّقُوهُنَّ لِعِدَّتِهِنَّ).

This is the first of five rulings mentioned in this verse. As commentators have derived from it, the meaning is that the formula of divorce should be pronounced at a time when the woman is clean from her menstrual period and has not had intercourse with her husband. This is because, according to Verse 228 of Surah Al-Baqarah, the waiting period (Iddah) for divorce must be "three menstrual cycles" (ثَلَاثَةَ قُرُوءٍ). Here, it emphasizes that the divorce must coincide with the beginning of the waiting period. This is only possible if the divorce occurs during a period of purity (Tuhr) without intercourse; for if the divorce occurs during menstruation, the start of the waiting period would be separate from the moment of divorce, and the Iddah would only begin after she becomes pure.

Similarly, if she is in a state of purity but has had intercourse with her husband, separation is still certain [to be restricted], because such purity—due to the intercourse—does not provide evidence of the absence of a fetus in the womb (reflect on this).

In any case, this is the first condition of divorce.

In numerous narrations from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is reported: "Whenever someone divorces his wife during her menstrual period, he must not pay heed to that divorce and should return until the woman becomes pure; then, if he wishes to divorce her, he may proceed." [1] This same meaning has been repeatedly mentioned in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, even cited as an interpretation of the verse. [2]

Then, it addresses the second ruling, which is the matter of keeping track of the waiting period, saying: "And keep count of the waiting period" (وَأَحْصُوا الْعِدَّةَ).

Observe precisely that the woman completes her periods of purity three times and sees her menstruation. When the third period of purity ends and she enters the third menstruation, the days of the Iddah have concluded.

If precision is not exercised in this matter, the Iddah period might be calculated as longer than necessary, causing harm to the woman by preventing her from remarrying. If it is shorter, the primary purpose of the Iddah—which is preserving the sanctity of the first marriage and ensuring no conception has occurred—is not observed.

"Ahsu" is derived from "Ihsa," meaning counting, and originally comes from "Hasa," meaning pebbles, because many people in ancient times who were not familiar with reading and writing kept track of various matters using pebbles.

It is noteworthy that those addressed to keep track of the Iddah are the men. This is because the "right to maintenance and housing" is their responsibility, and the "right of return" (Ruju') also belongs to them. Otherwise, women are also obligated to keep an accurate count of the Iddah to clarify their own status.

After this command, He invites all people to piety and God-wariness, saying: "And fear Allah, your Lord" (وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ رَبَّكُمْ). He is your Lord and Nourisher, and His commands are the guarantee of your happiness. Therefore, implement His orders and avoid disobeying Him, especially exercising precision in the matter of divorce and keeping track of the Iddah.

Then, He mentions the "third" and "fourth" rulings, one concerning husbands and the other concerning wives, saying: "Do not turn them out of their houses, nor shall they leave" (لَا تُخْرِجُوهُنَّ مِنْ بُيُوتِهِنَّ وَلَا يَخْرُجْنَ).

Although many uninformed people do not implement this Islamic ruling at the time of divorce at all—and as soon as the divorce formula is pronounced, the man allows himself to expel the woman, and the woman considers herself free to leave the husband's house and return to her relatives—this Islamic ruling has a very important philosophy. In addition to preserving the woman's dignity, it often prepares the ground for the husband to retract the divorce and strengthen the marital bond.

Turning one's back on this important Islamic ruling, which is stated in the text of the Holy Quran, causes many divorces to end in permanent separation, whereas if this ruling were implemented, it would often lead to reconciliation and a return to the marriage.

However, since circumstances sometimes arise where keeping the woman in the house after divorce is unbearable, He adds the fifth ruling as an exception, saying: "Unless they commit a clear indecency" (إِلَّا أَنْ يَأْتِينَ بِفَاحِشَةٍ مُبَيِّنَةٍ).

For example, she might be so incompatible, ill-tempered, and foul-mouthed toward her husband and his family that her continued presence in the home causes further problems.

This meaning is seen in numerous narrations reported from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. [1] Of course, this does not refer to every minor disagreement or incompatibility, as the concept of "Fahishah" implies a significant evil act, especially since it is described with the adjective "Mubayyinah" (clear/manifest).

There is also the possibility that "Fahishah" refers to "an act contrary to chastity," as reported in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام; in this case, "turning them out" would mean taking them out to carry out a legal penalty (Hadd) and then returning them to the house.

It is also possible to combine both meanings.

Following the statement of these rulings, He adds as an emphasis: "These are the limits of Allah; and whoever transgresses the limits of Allah has certainly wronged himself" (وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ وَمَنْ يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ اللَّهِ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُ).

This is because these divine laws and regulations guarantee the interests of the obligated individuals themselves, and transgressing them—whether by the man or the woman—damages their own happiness.

At the end of the verse, while making a subtle reference to the philosophy of the Iddah and the requirement for women not to leave their homes and primary residence, He says: "You know not; perhaps Allah will bring about after that a new situation" (لَا تَدْرِي لَعَلَّ اللَّهَ يُحْدِثُ بَعْدَ ذَلِكَ أَمْرًا).

With the passage of time, the storm of anger and rage—which often causes sudden decisions regarding divorce and separation—subsides. The permanent presence of the woman in the house alongside the man during the Iddah period, the reminder of the grim consequences of divorce (especially where children are involved), and the expression of affection by either party toward the other, creates the groundwork for a return (Ruju') and often clears the dark clouds of enmity and resentment from the sky of their lives.

Interestingly, in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

"The divorced woman [during her Iddah] may use kohl, dye her hair, use perfume, and wear whatever clothes she likes, for Allah, the Almighty and Sublime, says: 'Perhaps Allah will bring about after that a new situation,' so that she might perhaps capture his heart again and he might return to her." [1]

As we have said, the decision for separation and divorce is often influenced by fleeting emotions. With the passage of time and continuous interaction between the man and woman over a relatively long period (the Iddah period) and reflection on the outcome, the scene changes completely, and many separations end in reconciliation—provided that the aforementioned Islamic instructions, namely the woman staying in the former husband's house during the Iddah, are strictly implemented.

God willing, we will later mention that all of this pertains to "revocable divorce" (Talaq al-Raj'i).

Key Points

1- Divorce: The Most Hated of Permissible Things

Undoubtedly, the marriage contract is among those contracts that must be capable of dissolution, because sometimes causes arise that make the shared life of a man and woman impossible, or unbearable and full of corruption. If we were to insist that this contract remain forever, it would become a source of many problems. Therefore, Islam has agreed to the principle of divorce. We currently observe the result of the complete prohibition of divorce in Christian societies, where many men and women are legally considered spouses according to the distorted laws of the Christian religion, yet in practice, they live apart and each has even chosen an unofficial partner!

Therefore, the principle of divorce is a necessity, but a necessity that must be reduced to the minimum possible; as long as there is a way to continue the marriage, no one should resort to it.

For this reason, divorce is severely condemned in Islamic narrations and is referred to as the most hated of permissible things. As we read in a narration from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"There is nothing more hated by Allah, the Almighty and Sublime, than a house being destroyed in Islam by separation—meaning divorce." [1]

And in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Of the things Allah has made permissible, nothing is more hated to Him than divorce." [2]

Again, in another hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"Marry and do not divorce, for divorce causes the Throne ('Arsh) to tremble!" [3]

Why should it not be so, when divorce creates many problems for families, men, women, and especially children? These can be summarized mainly into three parts:

1- Emotional Problems: Undoubtedly, a man and woman who have lived together for years or months and then separate will be emotionally wounded. In a future marriage, the memory of the past marriage will constantly worry them, and they may even look at a future spouse with a kind of pessimism and suspicion. The harmful effects of this are hidden from no one, and thus it is often seen that such men and women renounce marriage forever.

2- Social Problems: Many women after divorce do not have a great chance for remarriage, at least not in a desirable and suitable way, and thus suffer severe loss. Even men, after divorcing their wives, will have a significantly lower chance of a desirable marriage, especially if children are involved. Therefore, they are often forced to settle for a marriage that does not truly satisfy them, suffering from this until the end of their lives.

3- Problems of the Children: This is the most important of all. It is rarely seen that stepmothers are as compassionate and kind as a mother, or able to fill the emotional void of children who have been torn from the loving embrace of their mother. The same applies to a stepfather if the former wife takes the child with her. Of course, there are men and women who are very loving and faithful toward children not their own, but their number is certainly small. For this reason, children after divorce suffer the greatest loss and damage, and perhaps most of them lose their mental health for the rest of their lives.

This is a tragedy not only for each family but for the entire society, because such children, deprived of the love of a mother or father, sometimes become dangerous individuals who, unconsciously influenced by a spirit of revenge, take their revenge on society as a whole.

If Islam has been so strict regarding divorce, the reason is these harmful effects in various dimensions.

For this same reason, the Holy Quran explicitly commands that whenever a dispute arises between a husband and wife, the relatives of both sides should strive to reconcile them, and through the formation of a "Family Peace Court," prevent the couple from being dragged to the Sharia court or to the issue of divorce and separation. (We have provided a description of the Peace Court in Volume 3, page 375 onwards, under Verse 35 of Surah Al-Nisa').

And again, for this reason, whatever helps the optimism of the man and woman and strengthens the foundations of family ties is desirable in Islam, and whatever destabilizes it is hated and loathed.

2- Motives for Divorce

Divorce, like any other social phenomenon, has various roots. Without careful study and addressing them, it is difficult to prevent such an occurrence. Therefore, before anything else, we must look for the factors of divorce and dry up its roots in society. These factors are many, the most important of which are:

A- Unlimited expectations of the woman or the man is one of the most important factors of separation. If each were to limit the scope of their expectations, step out of the world of dreams and fantasies, understand their partner well, and expect within the limits of what is possible, many divorces would be prevented.

B- The dominance of a spirit of luxury-seeking, extravagance, and wastefulness in families is another important factor that keeps women, in particular, in a state of constant dissatisfaction and clears the way for divorce through various excuses.

C- Undue interference by relatives, kin, and acquaintances in the private life of the couple, especially in their disputes, is considered another important factor. Experience has shown that if, during disputes between a couple, they are left to themselves and others do not fan the flames of the dispute by taking sides, it will not be long before it is extinguished. However, the interference of relatives, which is often accompanied by prejudice and misplaced affection, makes the matter more difficult and complicated day by day. Of course, this does not mean that relatives should always distance themselves from these disputes; rather, the point is to leave them to themselves in minor disputes, but whenever the dispute becomes general and deep-rooted, they should intervene with consideration for the interests of both parties, avoiding any one-sided or prejudiced stance, and facilitate their reconciliation.

D- Indifference of the man and woman to each other's desires, especially those related to emotional and sexual matters. For example, every man expects his wife to be clean and attractive, and every woman has the same expectation of her husband. However, these are matters they are often unwilling to express. This is where the indifference of the partner, failing to attend to one's appearance, neglecting necessary grooming, and being disheveled or dirty, makes the spouse weary of continuing such a marriage—especially if there are individuals in their environment who observe these matters while they pass by them with indifference.

Therefore, great importance is given to this in Islamic narrations. As we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"It is not fitting for a woman to leave herself unkempt [without adornment for her husband]." [1]

And in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, he said:

"Women have left the path of chastity for immorality, and nothing caused it but the lack of grooming by their husbands!" [2]

E- Incompatibility of family culture and the temperaments of the man and woman is also one of the important factors of divorce. This is an issue that must be carefully considered before choosing a spouse—that the two, in addition to being "Sharia peers" (Kufu'), i.e., Muslims, should also be "secondary peers," meaning that the necessary compatibilities in various aspects are observed between them. Otherwise, one should not be surprised by the breakdown of such marriages.

3- The Philosophy of Observing the Iddah

Undoubtedly, the "Iddah" has two basic philosophies mentioned in the Holy Quran and Islamic reports: First, the issue of preserving the lineage and clarifying the woman's status regarding pregnancy or non-pregnancy. Second, the existence of a means to return to the first life and eliminate the factors of separation, to which a subtle reference was made in the above verse—especially since Islam emphasizes that women must stay in the man's house during the Iddah period. Naturally, they will have a continuous interaction for several months, which gives them the opportunity to re-examine the issue of separation away from fleeting emotions.

Particularly in the case of "revocable divorce" (Talaq al-Raj'i) [1], where returning to the marriage does not require any formalities; any action or word that indicates the man's desire to return is considered a "return" (Ruju'). Even if he touches the woman's body with or without desire, even if he did not intend to return, it is considered a return.

In this way, if this period passes under the conditions mentioned above and the two do not reconcile, it becomes clear that they truly lack the readiness to continue a shared life, and it is in their best interest to separate.

In this regard, we have provided another explanation in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh under Verse 228 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

[1]: "Revocable divorce" refers to a divorce that occurs for the first or second time, where the decision for separation is from the man's side, such that the woman neither grants her dower (Mahr) nor any other property [to obtain the divorce].

Ayahs

فَإِذَا بَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ فَارِقُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ وَأَشْهِدُوا۟ ذَوَىْ عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَـٰدَةَ لِلَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ يُوعَظُ بِهِۦ مَن كَانَ يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مَخْرَجًا (٢)
Then, when they have completed their term, either retain them honourably or separate from them honourably, and take the witness of two honest men from among yourselves, and bear witness for the sake of Allah. Whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day is advised to [comply with] this. Whoever is wary of Allah, He shall make for him a way out [of the adversities of the world and the Hereafter] (2)
وَيَرْزُقْهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَحْتَسِبُ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَـٰلِغُ أَمْرِهِۦ ۚ قَدْ جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدْرًا (٣)
and provide for him from whence he does not count upon. And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him. Indeed Allah carries through His commands. Certainly, Allah has ordained a measure [and extent] for everything. (3)

Commentary

Either Reconciliation or a Gracious Parting

In continuation of the discussions regarding "divorce" mentioned in the previous verses, the first verse under discussion points to several other rulings. First, It states:

"Then when they have reached their [final] term, either retain them in a fair manner—through revocation of divorce—or part with them in a fair manner."

(فَإِذا بَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ فارِقُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ)

The meaning of "reaching the term" (بلوغ اجل) here is not that the 'iddah period has completely ended, but rather reaching the final days of the period. Otherwise, returning (recourse) after the completion of the 'iddah is not permissible, unless the retention occurs through a new marriage contract; however, this latter meaning seems very far-fetched from the context of the verse.

In any case, this verse presents one of the most important and calculated instructions regarding marital life: that a man and woman must either live together in a worthy manner or separate in a worthy manner. Just as communal life must be based on sound principles and humane, decent conduct, separation must also be free from any clamor, litigation, conflict, backbiting, insults, injustice, or violation of rights. It is vital that just as unions are formed with peace and sincerity, separations should also be accompanied by mutual understanding. This is because these two individuals might consider reuniting in the future, but if their conduct during separation was so foul that it darkened their mental atmosphere, the path to return would be blocked. Even if they wished to remarry, they would lack the appropriate intellectual and emotional foundation. Furthermore, both are ultimately Muslims belonging to the same society; a separation coupled with hostility and unseemly behavior not only affects them but also has detrimental effects on both families, sometimes completely destroying the grounds for future cooperation.

Truly, how excellent it would be if—not only in marriage but in every friendship and joint venture—a person continues cooperation worthily as long as possible, and whenever they cannot, they separate in a worthy manner. Indeed, a "worthy separation" is itself a type of victory and success for both parties! From what we have said, it becomes clear that "retention in a fair manner" (امساک به معروف) and "parting in a fair manner" (جدایی به معروف) have a broad meaning that encompasses all obligatory and recommended conditions and ethical programs, visualizing a collection of Islamic and moral etiquettes.

Then, referring to the second ruling, It adds: At the time of divorce and separation, take as witnesses two just men from among yourselves (from the Muslims). (وَ أَشْهِدُوا ذَوَيْ عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ)

So that if a dispute arises in the future, neither party can deny the facts.

Some commentators have suggested that taking witnesses applies to both divorce and recourse (return). However, since taking witnesses during recourse—or even during marriage—is certainly not obligatory, if we assume the verse includes recourse, it would be a recommended (mustahabb) instruction in that case.

In the third instruction, It defines the duty of the witnesses as follows: "And establish the testimony for Allah." (وَ أَقِيمُوا الشَّهادَةَ لِلَّهِ)

Lest your heart's inclination toward one of the two parties prevents you from testifying to the truth. No motive other than Allah and the establishment of truth should find its way into it. It is true that witnesses must be just, but despite being just, the occurrence of sin is not impossible; for this reason, He warns them to be watchful of themselves and not to deviate from the path of truth, whether consciously or unconsciously.

Furthermore, the expression "ذَوَيْ عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ" (two just ones from among you) is evidence that the two witnesses must be "Muslim," "just," and "male."

At the end of the verse, as an emphasis on all the preceding rulings, It adds:

"This is what is exhorted to whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day." (ذلِكُمْ يُوعَظُ بِهِ مَنْ كانَ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ)

Some have considered "this" (ذلکم) to refer only to the matter of turning to Allah and observing justice by the witnesses, but it appears that this expression has a broad meaning and encompasses all the previous rulings regarding divorce.

In any case, this expression is evidence of the extraordinary importance of these rulings, such that if someone does not observe them and does not take heed of this exhortation, it is as if they do not believe in Allah and the Last Day.

And since sometimes issues related to livelihood and future life, or other family troubles, cause the spouses during divorce or recourse, or the witnesses during testimony, to deviate from the path of truth and justice, at the end of the verse It says: "And whoever fears Allah and refrains from sin, He will make for him a way out and solve the problems of his life." (وَ مَنْ يَتَّقِ اللَّهَ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ مَخْرَجاً)

"And He will provide for him from where he does not expect." (وَ يَرْزُقْهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَحْتَسِبُ)

"And whoever relies upon Allah and entrusts his affairs to Him, He is sufficient for him." (وَ مَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ)

"For indeed Allah, the Almighty, His command is effective in all things, and whatever He wills, He brings to pass." (إِنَّ اللَّهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ)

"But Allah has appointed for every thing a measure and an account." (قَدْ جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدْراً)

In this way, He warns men, women, and witnesses not to fear the difficulties of the truth, to be executors of justice, and to seek the opening of closed matters from Allah; for Allah has guaranteed to open the problems of the God-fearing and to provide for them from where they themselves do not expect.

Allah has guaranteed that whoever relies on Him will not be left helpless, and Allah is capable of fulfilling this guarantee.

It is true that these verses were revealed regarding divorce and its related rulings, but they contain a vast content that includes all other cases. It is a promising pledge from Allah to all the God-fearing and those who rely on Him, that ultimately Divine grace will reach them, lead them through the twists and turns of difficulties, guide them toward the bright horizon of prosperity, remove the hardships of livelihood, and clear the dark clouds of problems from the sky of their lives.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 24, p. 235

The phrase "قَدْ جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدْراً" (Allah has appointed for every thing a measure) is a subtle reference to the system governing both legislation and creation. That is, these instructions that Allah has issued regarding divorce and other matters are all according to a precise and wise calculation and measurement. Likewise, the problems that occur throughout a person's life—whether in marriage or otherwise—each has a measure, an account, a wisdom, and an end. One should not panic when these events occur, nor open one's mouth in complaint, nor resort to lack of piety to solve problems. Rather, one should fight them with the power of piety and self-restraint and seek the final solution from Allah.

Key Points

1- Piety and Deliverance from Problems

The above verses are among the most promising verses of the Holy Quran; reciting them brings purity to the heart and light and radiance to the soul. They tear through the veils of despair and hopelessness, shine the life-giving rays of hope into the heart, and promise salvation and the resolution of problems to all God-fearing individuals.

In a hadith narrated from Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"I know a verse which, if all people were to hold fast to it, it would suffice them: 'And whoever fears Allah, He will make for him a way out...' and he continued to recite it and repeat it." [1]

In another hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that in the interpretation of this verse he said:

"From the doubts of the world, from the agonies of death, and the hardships of the Day of Resurrection!" [1]

This expression is evidence that the opening of affairs for the people of piety is not limited to this world but also includes the Hereafter.

And in another hadith from the same Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"Whoever increases his seeking of forgiveness (istighfar), Allah will grant him relief from every grief and a way out from every narrowness." [2]

A group of commentators have said: The first verse above was revealed regarding "Awf bin Malik," who was one of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The enemies of Islam captured his son. He came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and complained of this incident and his poverty and destitution. The Prophet said: "Practice piety, be patient, and frequently say the dhikr: La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah (There is no power nor strength except by Allah)." He did this, and suddenly, while he was sitting in his house, his son entered through the door. It turned out he had taken advantage of a moment of the enemy's negligence and escaped, even bringing a camel from the enemy with him. (It was here that the above verse was revealed, announcing the resolution of the problem of this pious individual and provision from where he did not expect). [3]

It is also necessary to mention that the concept of the verse is never that a person should forget effort and striving for life and say, "I will sit at home, practice piety, and recite La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah until provision reaches me from where I do not expect." No, the concept of the verse is never such. The goal is piety and God-fearing coupled with effort and striving. If, in this state, doors are closed to a person, Allah has guaranteed their opening.

Therefore, we read in a hadith that one of the companions of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, "Umar bin Muslim," did not come to him for a while. The Imam inquired about him. They said: "He has abandoned trade and turned to worship." He said: "Woe to him! Does he not know that the one who abandons seeking [livelihood], his prayer is not answered?"

Then he added: "A group of the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, when the verse 'And whoever fears Allah, He will make for him a way out and provide for him from where he does not expect' was revealed, closed their doors and turned to worship, saying: 'Allah has taken charge of our provision!' This news reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. He sent someone to them asking why they had done so. They said: 'O Messenger of Allah! Since Allah has guaranteed our provision, we occupied ourselves with worship.' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'Indeed, whoever does that, his prayer will not be answered; upon you is to seek [livelihood]!'" [1]

2- The Spirit of Reliance (Tawakkul)

The meaning of reliance on Allah (Tawakkul) is that a striving person entrusts his work to Him and seeks the solution to his problems from Him—the God who is aware of all his needs, the God who is Merciful and Kind to him, and the God who has the power to solve any problem.

One who possesses the spirit of reliance never allows despair and hopelessness to enter; he does not feel weak or abject in the face of problems; he is resilient against harsh events. This very culture and belief give him such psychological strength that he can overcome difficulties. On the other hand, the unseen aids promised to those who rely on Allah come to his assistance and save him from defeat and inability.

In a hadith from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated that he said: I asked the messenger of Allah's revelation, Gabriel: "What is Tawakkul?" He said:

"The knowledge that the created being neither harms nor benefits, neither gives nor withholds; and the employment of despair from the creation. When a servant is like that, he does not work for anyone other than Allah, does not hope in nor fear anyone other than Allah, and does not covet anyone other than Allah—this is Tawakkul." [1]

"Tawakkul" with this deep content grants a person a new personality and affects all his actions. Therefore, we read in a hadith that the noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, on the night of Mi'raj, asked of Allah: "O Lord! Which action is the most virtuous?"

Allah Almighty said: "There is nothing with Me more virtuous than reliance on Me and being pleased with what I have apportioned." [1]

It is obvious that Tawakkul in this sense is always coupled with struggle, effort, and striving, not laziness and fleeing from responsibilities.

We have provided another explanation in this regard in Volume 10, page 295 onwards (under verse 12 of Surah Ibrahim).

The same incident appears in the Tafsir of Fakhr Razi and Ruh al-Bayan with some differences; some of them record the number of camels as one hundred. Ayahs

وَٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى يَئِسْنَ مِنَ ٱلْمَحِيضِ مِن نِّسَآئِكُمْ إِنِ ٱرْتَبْتُمْ فَعِدَّتُهُنَّ ثَلَـٰثَةُ أَشْهُرٍ وَٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى لَمْ يَحِضْنَ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰتُ ٱلْأَحْمَالِ أَجَلُهُنَّ أَن يَضَعْنَ حَمْلَهُنَّ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ يُسْرًا (٤)
As for those of your wives who have ceased having menses—[or] if you have any doubts [concerning its cause, whether it’s is age or something else]—their term [of waiting] and of those who have not yet had menses, shall be three months. As for those who are pregnant, their term shall be until they deliver. And whoever is wary of Allah, He shall grant him ease in his affairs. (4)
ذَٰلِكَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ أَنزَلَهُۥٓ إِلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِۦ وَيُعْظِمْ لَهُۥٓ أَجْرًا (٥)
That is Allah’s ordinance, which He has sent down to you, and whoever is wary of Allah, He shall absolve him of his misdeeds and give him a great reward. (5)
أَسْكِنُوهُنَّ مِنْ حَيْثُ سَكَنتُم مِّن وُجْدِكُمْ وَلَا تُضَآرُّوهُنَّ لِتُضَيِّقُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ ۚ وَإِن كُنَّ أُو۟لَـٰتِ حَمْلٍ فَأَنفِقُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ حَتَّىٰ يَضَعْنَ حَمْلَهُنَّ ۚ فَإِنْ أَرْضَعْنَ لَكُمْ فَـَٔاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ ۖ وَأْتَمِرُوا۟ بَيْنَكُم بِمَعْرُوفٍ ۖ وَإِن تَعَاسَرْتُمْ فَسَتُرْضِعُ لَهُۥٓ أُخْرَىٰ (٦)
House them where you live, in accordance with your means, and do not harass them to put them in straits, and should they be pregnant, maintain them until they deliver. Then, if they suckle [the baby] for you, give them their wages and consult together honourably. But if you make things difficult for each other, then another woman will suckle [the baby] for him. (6)
لِيُنفِقْ ذُو سَعَةٍ مِّن سَعَتِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن قُدِرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقُهُۥ فَلْيُنفِقْ مِمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا مَآ ءَاتَىٰهَا ۚ سَيَجْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ بَعْدَ عُسْرٍ يُسْرًا (٧)
Let the affluent man spend out of his affluence, and let he whose provision has been tightened spend out of what Allah has given him. Allah does not task any soul except [according to] what He has given it. Allah will bring about ease after hardship. (7)

Commentary

Rulings on Divorced Women and Their Rights

Among the rulings derived from the preceding verses is the necessity of observing the waiting period ('iddah) after divorce. Since the ruling for women who have regular menstrual cycles was clarified in verse 228 of Surah Al-Baqarah—stating that they must pass three periods of purity—the current verses clarify the rulings for other groups who, for various reasons, do not menstruate or are pregnant, thereby completing the discussion on the waiting period.

First, He says: "And those of your women who have passed the age of menstruation, if you are in doubt (about their pregnancy), their waiting period is three months" (وَ اللَّائِي يَئِسْنَ مِنَ الْمَحِيضِ مِنْ نِسائِكُمْ إِنِ ارْتَبْتُمْ فَعِدَّتُهُنَّ ثَلاثَةُ أَشْهُرٍ).

"And also those who have not yet menstruated"—they too must observe a waiting period of three full months (وَ اللَّائِي لَمْ يَحِضْنَ‌). Then, referring to the third group, He adds: "And for those who are pregnant, their term is until they deliver their burden" (وَ أُولاتُ الْأَحْمالِ أَجَلُهُنَّ أَنْ يَضَعْنَ حَمْلَهُنَ‌).

Thus, the ruling for three additional groups of women is specified in this verse: two groups must observe a three-month waiting period, and the third group—pregnant women—ends their waiting period upon giving birth, whether that occurs one hour after the divorce or, for instance, eight months later.

Regarding the meaning of the phrase "if you are in doubt" (إِنِ ارْتَبْتُمْ‌), three possibilities have been mentioned:

  1. It refers to doubt regarding the existence of "pregnancy." This means that if there is a possibility of pregnancy in a woman who has reached the age of menopause (fifty years for ordinary women and sixty for Qurayshi women), she must observe the waiting period. Although this is rare, it does happen. (Note that the term ribah, meaning doubt in pregnancy, appears frequently in narrations and the words of jurists) [^1].
  2. It refers to women about whom it is not certain whether they have reached the age of menopause or not.
  3. It refers to doubt regarding the legal ruling itself. Thus, the verse would mean: "If you do not know God's ruling, His ruling is that such women must observe the waiting period." However, the first interpretation is the most appropriate, as the literal sense of the phrase "those who have passed the age of menstruation" implies they have reached menopause. Furthermore, whenever women's menstruation ceases due to illness or other factors, they fall under this same ruling—meaning they must observe a three-month waiting period. (This ruling can be derived through the principle of priority or by being included in the literal scope of the verse) [^2].

The phrase "and those who have not yet menstruated" (وَ اللَّائِي لَمْ يَحِضْنَ‌) may refer to those who have reached the age of puberty but do not menstruate; in this case, they must undoubtedly observe a three-month waiting period. Another possibility suggested in the interpretation of the verse is that it includes all women who have not menstruated, whether they have reached puberty or not.

However, the famous view among our jurists is that if a girl has not reached the age of puberty, she has no waiting period after divorce. Nevertheless, there are opponents to this view who argue based on certain narrations in this regard, and the literal appearance of the verse aligns with them. (A more detailed explanation of this issue should be studied in books of jurisprudence) [^1].

The interpretation above is also supported by the "circumstances of revelation" (shan al-nuzul) mentioned for the final clauses: Ubayy ibn Ka'b said to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله that the waiting period for some women is not mentioned in the Quran, including young girls, elderly women (menopausal), and pregnant women. Consequently, this verse was revealed to state their rulings [^2]. Furthermore, the waiting period applies if there is a possibility of pregnancy, as the verse conjoins them with menopausal women, implying their ruling is the same [^3]. Finally, at the end of the verse, He emphasizes the issue of taqwa (God-wariness) once more, saying:

"And whoever fears Allah, He will make his matter easy for him" (وَ مَنْ يَتَّقِ اللَّهَ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ يُسْراً).

Both in this world and the next, He will resolve his difficulties through His grace, whether they relate to the issue of separation and divorce and its rulings, or to other matters.

In the following verse, to further emphasize the rulings regarding divorce and the waiting period mentioned previously, He adds: "That is the command of Allah, which He has sent down to you" (ذلِكَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ أَنْزَلَهُ إِلَيْكُمْ‌). "And whoever fears Allah and avoids disobeying His command, He will remove his sins from him and enlarge his reward" (وَ مَنْ يَتَّقِ اللَّهَ يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّئاتِهِ وَ يُعْظِمْ لَهُ أَجْراً).

Some commentators have said that "sins" (sayyi'at) here refers to "minor sins," and taqwa refers to avoiding "major sins." Thus, avoiding major sins leads to the forgiveness of minor ones, similar to what is stated in Surah An-Nisa, verse 31. This implies that violating the aforementioned rulings regarding divorce and the waiting period is considered among the major sins [^1]. While it is true that sayyi'at is sometimes used to mean minor sins, in many Quranic verses, it refers to sins in general, both minor and major. For example, in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 65, we read: "And if the People of the Book had believed and feared Allah, We would have surely removed from them their misdeeds"—meaning We would forgive all their past sins. (This meaning appears in other verses as well). It is certain that faith and the acceptance of Islam cause the forgiveness of all prior sins.

The next verse provides more detail regarding the rights of the woman after separation, concerning "housing" and "maintenance" (nafaqah), as well as other aspects. First, regarding the housing of divorced women, He says: "Lodge them where you dwell, according to your means" (أَسْكِنُوهُنَّ مِنْ حَيْثُ سَكَنْتُمْ مِنْ وُجْدِكُمْ‌).

Wujd (rhyming with hukm) means ability and financial capacity. Some commentators have offered other interpretations, but they ultimately return to this same meaning. Raghib also states in Al-Mufradat that the expression "according to your means" (min wujdikum) implies providing suitable housing for divorced women in proportion to one's ability and wealth. Naturally, where housing is the husband's responsibility, the rest of the maintenance will also be his responsibility; the continuation of the verse, which speaks of the maintenance of pregnant women, also supports this claim.

Then, addressing another ruling, He says: "And do not harm them so as to distress them" (وَ لا تُضآرُّوهُنَّ لِتُضَيِّقُوا عَلَيْهِنَ‌)—forcing them to move out or forgo their maintenance. Let not grudges, enmity, or hatred divert you from the path of truth and justice, depriving them of their established rights to housing and maintenance, and placing them under such pressure that they abandon everything and flee!

In the third ruling regarding pregnant women, He says: "And if they are pregnant, then spend on them until they deliver their burden" (وَ إِنْ كُنَّ أُولاتِ حَمْلٍ فَأَنْفِقُوا عَلَيْهِنَّ حَتَّى يَضَعْنَ حَمْلَهُنَ‌). This is because as long as they have not given birth, they are in a state of iddah, and maintenance and housing are obligatory upon the husband.

In the fourth ruling regarding the rights of "nursing women," He says: "Then if they suckle the child for you, give them their recompense" (فَإِنْ أَرْضَعْنَ لَكُمْ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَ‌)—a wage appropriate to the amount and duration of nursing according to custom and habit.

Since infants and children often become the bargaining chips in disputes between two spouses after separation, He issues a decisive command in the fifth ruling: "And consult together kindly regarding the child's fate" (وَ أْتَمِرُوا بَيْنَكُمْ بِمَعْرُوفٍ‌). Let not the disputes of the parents harm the interests of the children, causing them physical loss or depriving them of necessary emotional love and compassion. Parents are duty-bound to keep Allah in mind and not sacrifice the interests of the defenseless infant for their own disputes and motives.

The word wa'tamiru from the root i'timar sometimes means "accepting a command" and sometimes "consultation"; here, the second meaning is more appropriate. The expression bi-ma'ruf (kindly/properly) is a comprehensive term covering any consultation that involves goodness and welfare.

Since the necessary agreement between the two spouses after divorce for the child's welfare and nursing is sometimes not achieved, He states in the sixth ruling: "But if you find it difficult for one another, then another woman may suckle the child for him" (وَ إِنْ تَعاسَرْتُمْ فَسَتُرْضِعُ لَهُ أُخْرى‌). This indicates that if disputes persist, do not remain idle; entrust the child to another. While it was primarily the mother's right to nurse the child, if this becomes impossible due to hardship and conflict, the child's interests must not be forgotten; he should be placed with a wet nurse.

The next verse states the seventh and final ruling in this regard, adding:

"Let the man of means spend according to his means; and he whose resources are restricted, let him spend according to what Allah has given him. Allah does not burden any soul except according to what He has given it" (لِيُنْفِقْ ذُو سَعَةٍ مِنْ سَعَتِهِ وَ مَنْ قُدِرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقُهُ فَلْيُنْفِقْ مِمَّا آتاهُ اللَّهُ لا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْساً إِلَّا ما آتاها).

Does this command—spending according to ability—refer to women who undertake nursing after separation, or to the days of the waiting period mentioned briefly in previous verses, or to both? The latter meaning is the most appropriate, although a group of commentators restricted it to nursing women, despite the fact that the previous verses used the term "wage" (ajr) rather than "maintenance" (nafaqah). In any case, those who have sufficient means should not be stingy or restrictive, and those who lack financial capacity are not commanded beyond their ability, and women cannot object to them. Thus, neither should those who have be miserly, nor are those who lack deserving of blame.

At the end of the verse, so that hardship in livelihood does not lead to straying from the path of truth and justice, and so that no one complains, He says: "Allah will soon bring about ease after hardship" (سَيَجْعَلُ اللَّهُ بَعْدَ عُسْرٍ يُسْراً). This means: do not grieve, do not be restless; the world does not remain in one state. Let not temporary and fleeting problems break the thread of your patience and endurance. This expression is a perpetual promise, especially at the time of the revelation of these verses when Muslims were under severe economic pressure—a glad tiding of a hopeful future for the patient. Indeed, it was not long before Allah opened the doors of His mercy and blessing to them.

Key Points

1. Rulings of "Revocable Divorce" (Talaq Raj'i)

We have stated that a "revocable divorce" is one in which the husband can return and re-establish the marital bond whenever he wishes, as long as the waiting period has not ended, without the need for a new contract. Interestingly, the return (ruju') is achieved with the slightest word or action indicating a return.

Some of the rulings mentioned in the above verses, such as maintenance and housing, are specific to the waiting period of a revocable divorce, as is the rule that the woman should not leave her husband's house during the iddah. However, in an irrevocable divorce (talaq ba'in)—one that cannot be revoked (such as the third divorce)—these rulings do not apply, except in the case of a pregnant woman, whose right to maintenance and housing remains until delivery.

The expression "You know not; perhaps Allah will bring about thereafter a new situation" also indicates that all or part of the above rulings pertain to revocable divorce [^1].

2. Allah Does Not Impose an Impossible Burden

Not only the judgment of reason but also the judgment of Sharia testifies that the duties of human beings must be within the limits of their ability. The phrase "Allah does not burden any soul except according to what He has given it" mentioned in the verses above also points to this meaning.

However, in some narrations, we read that "what He has given it" (ma ataha) means "what He has made known to it" (ma a'lamaha). Therefore, this verse has been used in the discussions of the "Principle of Innocence" (Asl al-Bara'ah) in the science of Principles of Jurisprudence (Usul), arguing that if a person does not know a ruling, he bears no responsibility for it. But since lack of knowledge sometimes causes lack of ability, it is possible that the intended meaning is ignorance that becomes a source of inability. Thus, the verse can have a broad meaning, encompassing both lack of power and ignorance that leads to the inability to perform a task.

3. The Importance of the Family System

The precision and delicacy used in stating the rulings of divorced women and their rights in the above verses—and even many of the intricacies of this issue being recounted in the verses of the Quran, which is effectively the constitution of Islam—is clear evidence of the importance Islam places on the family system and the preservation of the rights of women and children.

It prevents divorce as much as possible and dries up its roots; however, whenever matters reach a dead end and there is no choice but divorce and separation, it does not allow the rights of children or women to be trampled in this conflict. It even designs the plan for separation in such a way that the possibility of return often exists.

Commands such as "retaining with kindness" or "separating with kindness," as well as not causing harm, distress, or hardship to women, and proper consultation to clarify the fate of children, all testify to this meaning. Unfortunately, the lack of awareness among many Muslims regarding these matters, or the failure to act upon them despite awareness, has caused many problems for families and especially children during separation and divorce. This is only because Muslims have remained distant from the life-giving spring of the Quran. For example, although the Quran explicitly states that divorced women should not leave the husband's house during the waiting period, nor does the husband have the right to send them out—an act which, if performed, would greatly increase the hope of most women returning to married life—you will find few Muslim men or women who act upon this Islamic command after separation and divorce, and this is truly a cause for regret.

Ayahs

وَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ عَتَتْ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهَا وَرُسُلِهِۦ فَحَاسَبْنَـٰهَا حِسَابًا شَدِيدًا وَعَذَّبْنَـٰهَا عَذَابًا نُّكْرًا (٨)
How many a town defied the command of its Lord and His apostles, then We called it to a severe account and punished it with a dire punishment. (8)
فَذَاقَتْ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهَا وَكَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ أَمْرِهَا خُسْرًا (٩)
So it tasted the evil consequences of its conduct, and the outcome of its conduct was ruin. (9)
أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۚ قَدْ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْكُمْ ذِكْرًا (١٠)
Allah has prepared for them a severe punishment. So be wary of Allah, O you who possess intellect and have faith! Allah has already sent down to you a reminder, (10)
رَّسُولًا يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ مُبَيِّنَـٰتٍ لِّيُخْرِجَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۚ وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ قَدْ أَحْسَنَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُۥ رِزْقًا (١١)
an apostle reciting to you the manifest signs of Allah that He may bring out those who have faith and do righteous deeds from darkness into light. And whoever has faith in Allah and does righteous deeds, He shall admit him into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. Allah has certainly granted him an excellent provision. (11)

Commentary

The Painful End of the Rebellious

It is the method of the Quran in many instances that after mentioning a series of practical instructions, it refers to the state of previous nations, so that Muslims may see with their own eyes the results of "obedience" and "disobedience" in their history, and the matter may take on a sensory form.

Therefore, in this Surah as well, after mentioning the duties of men and women at the time of divorce and separation, it turns to this same meaning and warns the disobedient and the headstrong.

First, He says: "How many a township revolted against the command of its Lord and His messengers, so We called it to a severe account and punished it with a terrible punishment!" (وَ كَأَيِّنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ عَتَتْ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّها وَ رُسُلِهِ فَحاسَبْناها حِساباً شَدِيداً وَ عَذَّبْناها عَذاباً نُكْراً) [^1] The meaning of "township" (qaryah), as we have said before, is a gathering place of humans, whether a city or a village, and here it refers to its inhabitants.

Atat (عَتَتْ) is derived from the root utuww (عتو), meaning to rebel against obedience.

And nukr (نُكْر) means a difficult task that is unprecedented or rare.

"A severe account" (hisaban shadidan) either means a precise account accompanied by strictness, or it means a severe punishment which is the result of a precise account. In any case, it refers to the punishment of these rebellious peoples in this world, where some were uprooted by storms, some by devastating earthquakes, some by lightning and the like, and their ruined cities and lands remained as a lesson for future generations.

Therefore, in the next verse, He adds: "So it tasted the evil consequence of its conduct, and the end of its conduct was loss" (فَذاقَتْ وَبالَ أَمْرِها وَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ أَمْرِها خُسْراً). What loss is worse than losing God-given capitals, and in this market of worldly trade, not only did they buy no goods, but they were ultimately destroyed by divine punishment.

[^1]: According to the famous opinion of grammarians, ka-ayyin (كاين) is a compound noun consisting of the kaf of comparison and ay accompanied by tanwin. Since the tanwin has entered the structure of this noun, it is pronounced even when pausing (waqf), and it is written as such in the transcription of the Qurans. Its meaning is the same as the informative kam (meaning "how many"), although there is a slight difference between them. A less famous opinion is that it is a simple noun and its kaf and nun are part of the word itself.

Some have considered "severe account" and "terrible punishment" here as a reference to the "punishment of the Resurrection," taking the past tense verb to mean the future. However, there is no reason for this, especially since the punishment of the Resurrection will be discussed in the next verse, and this itself is living evidence that the punishment here is the punishment of this world.

Then, referring to their afterlife punishment, He says: "Allah has prepared for them a severe punishment" (أَعَدَّ اللَّهُ لَهُمْ عَذاباً شَدِيداً). A painful, severe, terrifying, humiliating, disgraceful, and eternal punishment in Hell is prepared for them even now.

Since this is the case, "So fear Allah, O men of understanding who have believed!" (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ يا أُولِي الْأَلْبابِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

Thought and intellect on one hand, and faith and divine verses on the other, warn you to see the fate of rebellious and tyrannical nations and take a lesson from it, lest you find yourselves in their ranks; for Allah will afflict you with a horrific and unprecedented punishment in this world, and a severe punishment in the Hereafter awaits you.

Then, addressing the thinking believers, He adds: "Allah has indeed sent down to you a reminder" (قَدْ أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ إِلَيْكُمْ ذِكْراً).

"And a Messenger who recites to you the clear verses of Allah to bring out those who believe and do righteous deeds from darknesses into the light" (رَسُولًا يَتْلُوا عَلَيْكُمْ آياتِ اللَّهِ مُبَيِّناتٍ لِيُخْرِجَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ).

There is discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by "Reminder" (dhikr) and who is meant by "Messenger" (rasul).

A group considers "Reminder" to mean the "Quran," while another group has interpreted it as the "Messenger of Allah" صلى الله عليه وآله, because he is the source of reminding and awakening the people. According to this interpretation, the "Messenger" mentioned afterward is the Prophet himself (and the sentence has no omitted parts), but the meaning of "sending down" here is Allah granting the existence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to the Ummah.

However, if we take "Reminder" to mean the Holy Quran, "Messenger" cannot be an appositive for it, and there is an omitted part in this sentence, which would be estimated as: "Allah sent down to you a Reminder and sent a Messenger."

Some have interpreted "Messenger" to mean "Gabriel," in which case his descent would be a literal descent since he descended from heaven. However, this interpretation is not consistent with the phrase "recites to you the verses of Allah," because Gabriel does not directly recite the divine verses to the believers.

In short, each of these interpretations has its "merit" and "difficulty," but overall, the first interpretation (Reminder meaning the Quran and Messenger meaning the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله) is the best of all. This is because in many verses of the Holy Quran, the word dhikr is applied to the Quran, especially where it is accompanied by the word "sending down" (inzal), such that whenever "the sending down of the Reminder" is mentioned, it brings the Quran to mind.

In verse 44 of Surah An-Nahl, we read: "And We sent down to you the Reminder that you may make clear to the people what was sent down to them" (وَ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ). And in verse 6 of Surah Al-Hijr, it says: "And they said: O you upon whom the Reminder has been sent down, indeed you are mad!" (وَ قالُوا يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ). If it has appeared in some narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام that the "Reminder" refers to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and "We are the People of the Reminder" (Ahl al-Dhikr), it may refer to the inner depths (batn) of the verse. For we know that "the People of the Reminder" mentioned in the verse "So ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know" (فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ) (Nahl 43) does not exclusively mean the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام; rather, its occasion of revelation refers to the scholars of the People of the Book. However, given that "Reminder" has a broad meaning that also includes the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, this is considered one of its instances. [^1] In any case, the ultimate goal of sending this Messenger and revealing this Heavenly Book is that by reciting the divine verses, he may bring them out from the darknesses of disbelief, ignorance, sin, and moral corruption toward the light of faith, monotheism, and piety. In truth, all the goals of the Prophet's mission and the revelation of the Quran are summarized in this one phrase: bringing out from darknesses into the light. It is noteworthy that "darknesses" (zulumat) is mentioned in the plural form and "light" (nur) in the singular, because polytheism, disbelief, and corruption are factors of dispersion and multiplicity, while faith, monotheism, and piety are factors of unity and oneness.

[^1]: For further explanation, please refer to Volume 11 of Tafsir Nemouneh (page 243 onwards).

At the end of the verse, referring to the reward of those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He adds: "And whoever believes in Allah and does righteous deeds—and continues on this path—He will admit him into gardens beneath which rivers flow, to abide therein forever. Allah has indeed granted him a goodly provision" (وَ مَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللَّهِ وَ يَعْمَلْ صالِحاً يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً قَدْ أَحْسَنَ اللَّهُ لَهُ رِزْقاً).

The use of "believes" (yu'min) and "does" (ya'mal) in the present/future tense indicates that their faith and righteous deeds are not limited to a specific time but are continuous and ongoing. [^1] The expression "abiding" (khalidina) is proof of the eternity of Paradise; therefore, the mention of the word "forever" (abada) after it is considered an emphasis on "eternity" (khulud).

The expression "provision" (rizqan) in the indefinite form points to the greatness and importance of the goodly provisions that Allah prepares for this group. It has a broad concept that encompasses every kind of divine blessing in the Hereafter and even in this world, for the result of faith and piety is not related only to the Hereafter; believers and the God-fearing also have a purer, more peaceful, and more enjoyable life in this world.

[^1]: It should be noted that some of the pronouns used in the verse are in the plural form and some are in the singular form, and this is because where it is singular, it refers to the genus and the collective group.

Ayah

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ وَمِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ مِثْلَهُنَّ يَتَنَزَّلُ ٱلْأَمْرُ بَيْنَهُنَّ لِتَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ أَحَاطَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًۢا (١٢)
It is Allah who has created seven heavens, and of the earth [a number] similar to them. The command gradually descends through them, that you may know that Allah has power over all things, and that Allah comprehends all things in knowledge. (12)

Commentary

The Purpose of the Creation of the Universe is Knowledge

This verse, which is the final verse of Surah al-Talaq, contains a clear and meaningful reference to the greatness of Allah’s power in the creation of the heavens and the earth, as well as the ultimate purpose of this creation. It completes the discussions found in the preceding verses regarding the promise of a great reward for the God-fearing believers, as well as the promises given to resolve their difficulties. It is evident that the God Who possesses the power for this magnificent creation also has the ability to fulfill those promises in this world and the Hereafter.

First, He says: "Allah is He Who created seven heavens" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ).

"And of the earth the like thereof" (وَمِنَ الْأَرْضِ مِثْلَهُنَّ).

This means that just as the heavens are "sevenfold," the earths are also sevenfold; and this is the only verse in the Holy Quran that refers to seven earths.

Now let us see what is meant by these seven heavens and the similar earths. In this regard, we have had detailed discussions in Volume 1 under verse 29 of Surah al-Baqarah and in Volume 20 under verse 12 of Surah Fussilat; therefore, we will suffice here with a brief summary, which is as follows:

It is possible that the number seven refers to "multiplicity," as this expression is sometimes used in the Holy Quran and elsewhere to mean a large number. For example, we say, "Even if you bring seven seas, it would not be enough."

Accordingly, the meaning of seven heavens and seven earths would be the vast and numerous celestial bodies and planets similar to the earth.

However, if we take the number seven to denote a specific "quantity" and count, its meaning would be the existence of seven heavens. Considering verse 6 of Surah al-Saffat which says: إِنَّا زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِزِينَةٍ الْكَواكِبِ—"Indeed, We have adorned the nearest heaven (the first heaven) with the adornment of the stars"—it becomes clear that everything we see and all that human science encompasses belongs to the first heaven. Beyond these fixed stars and planets, there exist six other worlds that are beyond the reach of our current science.

This pertains to the seven heavens. As for the seven earths, it may refer to the different layers of the globe, as it has been proven today that the earth is composed of various strata. Or it may refer to the seven climes of the earth, as both in the past and today, the globe has been divided into seven "zones" (though the method of division differs; today the earth is divided into two arctic zones, two temperate zones, two tropical zones, and one equatorial zone, whereas in the past, the seven climes were divided differently).

However, it is also possible that here too, the number seven—derived from the expression "the like thereof" (مثلهن)—is for "multiplicity" and refers to the numerous earths that exist in the universe. To the extent that some astronomers say the planets similar to Earth that revolve around suns in this great universe number at least three hundred million! [1] Although it is difficult to provide an exact number given our limited information beyond the solar system, other astronomers also emphasize that in the galaxy of which the solar system is a part, there are millions upon millions of planets situated in conditions similar to Earth, serving as centers of life.

Of course, the advancement of human science in the future may provide us with more information regarding the interpretation of such verses.

Then, referring to the administration of this great universe by Allah’s command, He adds: "His command descends among them continuously" (يَتَنَزَّلُ الْأَمْرُ بَيْنَهُنَّ).

It is clear that "command" (امر) here refers to the creative command (amr takwini) of Allah regarding the administration of this vast universe and the seven heavens and earths, for He continuously guides and leads them in an organized path through His specific command. In fact, this verse is similar to verse 5 of Surah al-Sajdah which says:

[1]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, vol. 28, p. 151. In a Hadith narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we also read: "These stars that are in the sky are cities like the cities on earth" (Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 4, p. 15).

يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ مِنَ السَّماءِ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ—"He regulates the affair from the heaven to the earth."

In any case, if His administration were withdrawn from this world for a single moment, the entire system would collapse and head toward annihilation.

Finally, pointing to the goal of this magnificent creation, He says: "This is so that you may know that Allah has power over all things and that Allah encompasses all things in knowledge" (لِتَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ قَدْ أَحاطَ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عِلْماً).

What a beautiful expression, considering the goal of this great creation to be man's awareness of Allah’s attributes—His knowledge and His power—for awareness of these two attributes is sufficient for the cultivation and upbringing of a human being.

Man must know that He encompasses all the secrets of his existence and is aware of all his deeds. He must also know that His promises regarding the Resurrection, reward and punishment, and the victory of the believers are unfailing.

Indeed, the God Who possesses such "knowledge" and "power" and manages the system of the universe—if He has established laws regarding human life, divorce, and the rights of women, they are all based on a precise and solid calculation.

Regarding the "purpose of creation," we have had a detailed discussion under verse 56 of Surah al-Dhariyat in Volume 22 of Tafsir Nemouneh.

It is noteworthy that in various verses of the Quran, there are references to the purpose of the creation of man or the entirety of this world which may seem different at first glance, but upon closer inspection, we see they all return to a single reality:

  1. In verse 56 of Surah al-Dhariyat, the purpose of the creation of man and jinn is considered "worship" (وَ ما خَلقتُ الْجِنَّ وَ الْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ).
  2. In verse 7 of Surah Hud, the purpose of the magnificent creation of the heavens and the earth is introduced as the testing of mankind (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَ كانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْماءِ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا).
  3. In verse 119 of Surah Hud, the purpose is stated as Divine Mercy (وَ لِذلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ).
  4. And finally, in the verse under discussion, the purpose is stated as knowledge and awareness of Allah’s attributes (لِتَعْلَمُوا...).

A little reflection on these verses shows that some are a prelude to others: awareness and knowledge (ma'rifah) are a prelude to servitude and worship; that in turn is a prelude to the testing and cultivation of man; and that is a prelude to benefiting from Allah’s mercy (reflect on this).

O Allah! Now that You have acquainted us with the purpose of Your great creation, help us in attaining this grand goal! O Lord! Your knowledge and power are infinite, and Your mercy is also boundless; grant us a share of this infinite mercy! O God! You revealed the Quran and sent the Prophet to bring the believers out of darkness into light; bring us out of the darkness of sin and carnal desires, and illuminate our hearts with the light of faith and Taqwa. Amin, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah al-Talaq 28 Ramadan 1406
